summary: Sir, a simple cylindrical spacer is the sensible creature here: 18 mm outer diameter, 8 mm through-bore, 12 mm long. For a load-bearing spacer, keep the walls plain and the edges lightly chamfered or rounded later by hand so nothing mischievous starts cracking under compression.
parameters: clearance = 0.2; // optional print/process margin, adjust to suit axle fit
fit_checks: Outer diameter 18 mm with 8 mm bore leaves a 5 mm radial wall, which is perfectly respectable for a load-bearing spacer. Length 12 mm should seat cleanly if the mating faces are flat and the axle is actually 8 mm rather than “nominally, emotionally, approximately 8 mm.”

Check axle fit against your real shaft diameter and process tolerance. If this is 3D printed, the 8 mm bore may want a slight process clearance, typically 0.1 to 0.3 mm depending on printer behaviour and your appetite for filing.
export_detail: Exported CadQuery spacer assets as STL, STEP, and 3MF, then assembled a slicer pack for creality-k2-pro-combo using the fit-check-fast profile.

The riser is a crude vertical block at the rear edge; it is suitable as a rough placeholder, but you will likely want to replace it with a true angled support or fastener interface once the mounting method is confirmed.

summary: Sir, I have prepared a rough parameter-driven bracket concept suitable for later hand refinement. The mounting geometry is preserved at 150 mm hole spacing, with a 30 mm plate width and 8 mm thickness. I have added drainage intent and softened corners to reduce stress concentration, because metal, unlike optimism, does not enjoy sharp corners.
parameters: hole_diameter = 9; // reasonable default, revise to suit fastener
edge_margin = 15; // keeps holes away from plate ends
fit_checks: Mounting geometry is preserved by keeping the 150 mm hole spacing as the governing dimension. The 30 mm width is quite narrow, so hole diameter and edge margin should be checked against the actual fastener head size and washer footprint before fabrication.

Drainage is included as a central slot, but its exact placement should be confirmed so it does not weaken the highest-stress region near the bend or hole line. Corner radii are softened to improve fatigue resistance, and a bend relief is suggested at the formed transition if this is a bent part rather than a flat plate.

The main geometric risk is that a 30 mm wide plate leaves little room for generous hole edge distance. If you want, I can tighten this into a more realistic formed-bracket profile with specific leg lengths and bend orientation.
export_detail: Exported CadQuery bracket assets as STL, STEP, and 3MF, then assembled a slicer pack for creality-k2-pro-combo using the fit-check-fast profile.

summary: Sir, this is a rough parameter-driven bracket concept for later refinement. I have preserved the mounting hole spacing, kept the plate narrow at 30 mm, respected the 8 mm thickness, and added drainage and softened corners so the part is less keen to fail in a damp garden with unrealistic optimism.
parameters: hole_diameter = 9; // assume M8 clearance; revise as needed
end_margin = 15; // conservative default for edge distance
fit_checks: Hole spacing is held at 150 mm centre-to-centre. Plate width remains 30 mm, so edge distance is tight if the holes are large; for M8 clearance, 15 mm margins are workable but not generous. Bend radius of 12 mm is sensible against an 8 mm sheet, though the actual formed geometry still needs material-specific allowance.

The drainage hole is intentionally offset from the main fasteners so it does not weaken the critical mounting region. External corners are softened through the rounded outline, which is the polite engineering version of “less stress, fewer cracks.” If you want, I can tighten this into a proper folded L-bracket or convert it into a laser-cut flat pattern with bend allowances.
export_detail: Exported CadQuery bracket assets as STL, STEP, and 3MF, then assembled a slicer pack for creality-k2-pro-combo using the fit-check-fast profile.

summary: Sir, I have drafted a rough parameter-driven bracket concept suitable for hand refinement. The mounting geometry is preserved at 150 mm hole spacing, with a 30 mm plate width, 8 mm thickness, and a 12 mm bend radius. I have also introduced drainage and softened external corners to reduce stress concentration. A small triumph over rust and fatigue, which are usually very committed.
fit_checks: Mounting geometry: hole spacing set to 150 mm, retained symmetrically.
Plate width: 30 mm, as requested.
Thickness: 8 mm, represented directly in the model.

Bend radius: 12 mm, noted as a parameter for later bend or fillet treatment. The stub is intentionally approximate, because OpenSCAD is cheerful about pretending sheet-metal bends are cubes until one demands honesty.
Drainage: added via small through-holes at low points.
Fatigue resistance: external corners rounded with corner_r = 4 mm; increase if the bracket sees vibration or heavy cyclic loading.
Clearance check: confirm hole_dia against actual bolt size and washer diameter before cutting steel.
